content.text: Usage policies for agentic AI define what users and organizations are permitted to do with an agent system, under what conditions, and subject to what resource constraints. They are the operational contracts that govern access: who can call which tools, how many times, at what cost, and on what data — enforced programmatically at every request boundary.
What It Is

A usage policy is a machine-enforced rule set that governs how an agent system is accessed and operated. Usage policies address several distinct dimensions simultaneously: rate and quota limits (preventing resource exhaustion), scope limits (restricting which tools and data classes are accessible), content limits (preventing generation of prohibited content), cost limits (capping per-user or per-organization spending), and temporal limits (restricting agent activity to business hours or outside maintenance windows).

Unlike compliance policies (which enforce external regulatory obligations), usage policies primarily enforce the operator's internal rules about fair use, cost control, and tiered access. They answer questions like: Can this free-tier user call the code execution tool? Can this organization's agent access production databases? Has this user exhausted their daily token budget?

Usage policies are implemented at the application layer (not the network layer) because they require semantic context — knowing what tool is being called, which data it touches, and who the calling user is. A network-level firewall cannot make these decisions; an application-level policy engine can.
Why It Matters

Without usage policies, a single heavy user or misconfigured agent can exhaust the system's resources for all other users. LLM API calls are expensive (often $0.01–$0.10 per 1,000 tokens for frontier models), tool calls have real-world side effects, and agentic loops can multiply resource consumption by 10–100x compared to single-shot queries. Usage policies protect the system's economic viability and operational stability.

Usage policies also protect users from themselves and from misuse by third parties. An agent with unrestricted tool access can inadvertently delete production data, exhaust API quotas of downstream services, or generate costs an organization did not budget for. Explicit scope and cost limits create guardrails that prevent runaway agents from causing large-scale harm.

For multi-tenant systems (where multiple organizations or users share infrastructure), usage policies are the technical mechanism that makes tiered pricing and service differentiation viable. Free-tier users get lower quotas and restricted tool access; enterprise customers get higher limits and access to more powerful tools.
Benefits
Cost control: per-user and per-organization spending caps prevent unexpected bills from runaway agentic loops or adversarial prompt injection attacks
Fair use: rate limits prevent individual users from monopolizing shared infrastructure during high-demand periods
Tiered access: different user tiers get different quotas and tool access, enabling a freemium business model for agent platforms
Security: scope limits reduce the blast radius of compromised accounts — a breached free-tier user cannot call high-privilege tools
Operational stability: quotas prevent any single tenant from saturating LLM API capacity or downstream service rate limits
Predictable performance: rate limiting ensures consistent response latency for all users rather than allowing a few heavy users to degrade service for everyone
Trade-offs
User friction: overly aggressive rate limits block legitimate heavy users; finding the right thresholds requires data about real usage patterns
Implementation complexity: policies must be evaluated on every request; synchronous policy checks add latency; distributed rate limiting (across multiple server instances) requires shared state
Policy drift: as the system evolves, usage policy definitions can become stale and inconsistent with the current tool catalog and user tier structure
Edge cases: complex policies with many dimensions (rate × scope × cost × time) create many interaction effects that are difficult to test exhaustively
Operator override needs: legitimate administrative operations (batch migrations, emergency incident response) often need policy bypass; designing a secure override mechanism is non-trivial
Grandfathering conflicts: changing policy limits can break existing integrations for users who built workflows against the old limits
How It Works

Usage policy enforcement follows a middleware pattern. Every agent request passes through a policy evaluator before reaching the agent runtime. The evaluator receives the user's identity and role, the requested action (which tool, which parameters), and the user's current quota usage, then produces an allow/deny/modify decision.

Rate limiting is implemented with a sliding window or token bucket algorithm stored in Redis. On each request, the evaluator atomically reads the current usage counter, checks it against the limit, and if allowed, increments the counter. The counter expires automatically after the window duration. Distributed deployments use Redis Cluster to ensure consistent counter state across all policy evaluator instances.

Scope policies are evaluated as a set membership test: is the requested tool in the set of tools permitted for this user's tier? Tool permission sets are stored in the policy database and cached in memory with a short TTL (30–60 seconds) to avoid per-request database queries.

Cost policies maintain a spending ledger per user per billing period. After each tool call, the actual cost (tokens consumed, external API call cost) is written to the ledger asynchronously. Before each call, the synchronous check tests estimated cost against the remaining budget. Because cost is estimated before execution, a small overage buffer (typically 10%) is permitted to accommodate estimation errors.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Usage Policy Enforcement Architecture: User → Policy Evaluator (rate/quota/scope/content/cost/time) → ALLOW or DENY → Agent Runtime or Error

content.text: Every request from a user or organization enters the Policy Evaluator, which simultaneously checks rate limits, scope permissions, content rules, cost budgets, and time-window restrictions. Allowed requests proceed to the Agent Runtime; denied requests return an error and are logged to the Usage Ledger. The Usage Ledger maintains counters and billing data consumed by dashboards and the next round of evaluations.
When to Use It

Usage policies are appropriate for any agent system with more than one user, any system with real costs per operation, or any system that exposes potentially dangerous tools. In practice, this means usage policies should be implemented from the start of any production deployment, not added later.

Minimal usage policies (simple rate limits and scope restrictions) can be implemented in a few hours using Redis and a middleware decorator. More sophisticated policies (tiered access, cost budgets, content filtering) require dedicated policy infrastructure but are still far cheaper to implement proactively than to add after a misuse incident.

Internal tools with a single trusted user or a small known team may operate without formal usage policies. Once the user base grows beyond a handful of trusted individuals, or once the agent has access to expensive external APIs, usage policies become necessary.
How to Implement
Define the policy model: enumerate the dimensions (rate, scope, cost, content, time) and the user tiers; document the limits for each tier × dimension combination
Implement rate limiting: use Redis with atomic INCR + EXPIRE or a sliding window lua script; handle both per-user and per-organization limits
Implement scope checking: store tool permission sets per tier in a database; cache them in memory; add a middleware check before every tool dispatch
Implement cost tracking: estimate cost per tool call type; maintain a per-user spending ledger; enforce soft limits (warning) and hard limits (block)
Add policy decision logging: every allow/deny decision should be written to a log for audit and tuning purposes
Build a policy admin interface: operators need to view current usage, adjust limits, grant temporary exceptions, and override limits for specific users

# usage_policy.py
import time
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import redis

@dataclass
class UserContext:
    user_id: str
    org_id: str
    tier: str           # "free", "pro", "enterprise"
    roles: list[str]

@dataclass
class PolicyLimits:
    requests_per_minute: int
    tokens_per_day: int
    max_cost_per_day_usd: float
    allowed_tools: set[str]
    content_topics_blocked: list[str]
    active_hours: Optional[tuple[int,int]] = None  # (start_hour, end_hour) UTC

# Policy definitions per tier
TIER_POLICIES = {
    "free": PolicyLimits(
        requests_per_minute=10,
        tokens_per_day=50_000,
        max_cost_per_day_usd=1.0,
        allowed_tools={"search", "summarize", "qa"},
        content_topics_blocked=["financial_advice", "medical_diagnosis"]
    ),
    "pro": PolicyLimits(
        requests_per_minute=60,
        tokens_per_day=500_000,
        max_cost_per_day_usd=20.0,
        allowed_tools={"search", "summarize", "qa", "code_exec", "file_read"},
        content_topics_blocked=[]
    ),
    "enterprise": PolicyLimits(
        requests_per_minute=600,
        tokens_per_day=5_000_000,
        max_cost_per_day_usd=500.0,
        allowed_tools={"*"},  # all tools
        content_topics_blocked=[],
        active_hours=None  # no restriction
    )
}

class UsagePolicyEngine:
    def __init__(self, redis_client: redis.Redis):
        self.redis = redis_client

    def check_and_record(
        self,
        user: UserContext,
        tool_name: str,
        estimated_tokens: int = 0,
        estimated_cost_usd: float = 0.0
    ) -> tuple[bool, str]:
        """Check policy and return (allowed, reason)."""
        policy = TIER_POLICIES.get(user.tier)
        if not policy:
            return False, f"Unknown tier: {user.tier}"

        # 1. Scope check
        if "*" not in policy.allowed_tools and tool_name not in policy.allowed_tools:
            return False, f"Tool '{tool_name}' not permitted for {user.tier} tier"

        # 2. Time window check
        if policy.active_hours:
            hour = time.gmtime().tm_hour
            start, end = policy.active_hours
            if not (start <= hour < end):
                return False, f"Agent activity restricted to hours {start}-{end} UTC"

        # 3. Rate limit check (per-minute sliding window)
        rate_key = f"rate:{user.user_id}:{int(time.time())//60}"
        current = self.redis.incr(rate_key)
        self.redis.expire(rate_key, 120)  # 2 min TTL
        if current > policy.requests_per_minute:
            return False, f"Rate limit exceeded: {policy.requests_per_minute} req/min"

        # 4. Token quota check (per-day)
        today = time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        token_key = f"tokens:{user.user_id}:{today}"
        current_tokens = int(self.redis.get(token_key) or 0)
        if current_tokens + estimated_tokens > policy.tokens_per_day:
            return False, f"Daily token quota exceeded ({policy.tokens_per_day:,} tokens/day)"
        self.redis.incrby(token_key, estimated_tokens)
        self.redis.expire(token_key, 86400 * 2)

        # 5. Cost budget check (per-day)
        cost_key = f"cost:{user.org_id}:{today}"
        current_cost = float(self.redis.get(cost_key) or 0.0)
        if current_cost + estimated_cost_usd > policy.max_cost_per_day_usd:
            return False, f"Daily cost limit ${policy.max_cost_per_day_usd:.2f} exceeded"
        self.redis.incrbyfloat(cost_key, estimated_cost_usd)
        self.redis.expire(cost_key, 86400 * 2)

        return True, "allowed"

    def get_usage_summary(self, user_id: str) -> dict:
        """Return current usage counters for a user."""
        today = time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        minute = int(time.time()) // 60
        return {
            "requests_this_minute": int(self.redis.get(f"rate:{user_id}:{minute}") or 0),
            "tokens_today": int(self.redis.get(f"tokens:{user_id}:{today}") or 0),
        }
Examples
Middleware Integration

# Wrap tool dispatch with usage policy check
from usage_policy import UsagePolicyEngine, UserContext

policy_engine = UsagePolicyEngine(redis_client)

def dispatch_tool(tool_name: str, tool_inputs: dict, user: UserContext) -> dict:
    # Estimate cost before execution
    estimated_tokens = estimate_tokens(tool_inputs)
    estimated_cost = estimated_tokens / 1000 * 0.002  # $0.002 / 1K tokens

    allowed, reason = policy_engine.check_and_record(
        user=user,
        tool_name=tool_name,
        estimated_tokens=estimated_tokens,
        estimated_cost_usd=estimated_cost
    )
    if not allowed:
        raise PermissionError(f"Usage policy violation: {reason}")

    return execute_tool(tool_name, tool_inputs)
Quota Dashboard Query
Real-World Example

A developer tools company offers a cloud-based coding agent (similar to GitHub Copilot Workspace) with three tiers: Free (10 req/min, 50K tokens/day), Pro ($20/month, 60 req/min, 500K tokens/day), and Enterprise (custom SLA, metered billing). Free users can run the search and code review tools; Pro users additionally get the terminal execution tool (sandboxed); Enterprise customers get all tools including production database access.

The policy engine runs as a Redis-backed middleware service deployed in each region. Policy definitions are stored in a PostgreSQL database and cached in Redis with a 30-second TTL. When a Pro user attempts to use the database tool (an Enterprise-only tool), the scope check blocks the request and returns a 403 with a message directing them to upgrade. When a Free user hits the 10 req/min limit, they receive a 429 with a Retry-After header.

The billing team uses the cost ledger data (aggregated from Redis to PostgreSQL hourly) to generate invoices and detect usage anomalies (a single user consuming 50× their typical daily tokens triggers an alert for potential prompt injection or infinite loop).

# anomaly_detection.py — usage spike alert
import statistics

def detect_usage_spike(user_id: str, db) -> bool:
    # Get last 30 days of daily token usage
    history = db.query(
        "SELECT tokens_used FROM daily_usage "
        "WHERE user_id=%s ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT 30",
        (user_id,)
    )
    if len(history) < 7:
        return False  # not enough history
    values = [row["tokens_used"] for row in history]
    mean = statistics.mean(values[1:])  # exclude today
    std = statistics.stdev(values[1:])
    today = values[0]
    # Alert if today is more than 3 standard deviations above mean
    if std > 0 and (today - mean) / std > 3.0:
        send_alert(f"Usage spike detected for {user_id}: {today:,} tokens vs mean {mean:.0f}")
        return True
    return False
Common Mistakes
Single global rate limit: using one limit for all users rather than per-user limits allows a single heavy user to exhaust the global budget
No rate limit on admin endpoints: admin API routes that bypass normal usage policies can be exploited; all routes need rate limiting

Synchronous cost checks without a buffer: estimated costs differ from actual costs; hard-blocking when estimated cost exceeds the limit causes false denials; use an overage buffer (10%) and reconcile async
Quota resets at fixed midnight: users who know the reset time will burst immediately after midnight; stagger resets by user creation time to distribute load
No policy for anonymous/unauthenticated requests: even unauthenticated requests should be rate-limited by IP to prevent abuse before authentication completes
Not surfacing usage data to users: users who cannot see their current quota consumption cannot plan their usage; a usage dashboard reduces support tickets and frustrated users
Best Practices
Use atomic Redis operations (INCR + EXPIRE in a Lua script) for rate limit counters to prevent race conditions in distributed deployments
Implement both soft limits (warning, rate throttle) and hard limits (block) so users get notice before their requests are fully blocked
Cache policy definitions with a short TTL (30–60 seconds) to avoid per-request database queries without using stale policies for too long
Build a policy admin API that allows operators to temporarily raise limits for specific users without code deploys
Expose usage data to users via API or dashboard — transparency about limits and current consumption reduces support burden
Log every policy decision (allow and deny) to a queryable store; this data is essential for tuning limits and identifying misuse patterns
Performance & Scalability

Redis-based rate limiting adds approximately 1–3ms of latency per request when the Redis instance is co-located (same datacenter). Across datacenter boundaries, expect 10–30ms. For latency-critical paths, use a local in-memory counter with periodic async synchronization to Redis — this reduces latency to microseconds at the cost of allowing brief overage during synchronization intervals.

At very high request volumes (>100,000 requests/second per Redis instance), consider Redis Cluster partitioned by user_id hash to distribute counter writes. Each partition handles a subset of users, scaling linearly with the number of partitions.

Policy definition lookups are typically 10–100x less frequent than rate limit checks (they change only when a user's tier changes), making them ideal candidates for aggressive in-memory caching. A LRU cache with a 60-second TTL and capacity for 10,000 users adds negligible memory overhead on each application server while nearly eliminating policy database reads.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Redis / Redis Cluster
Rate limit counters, quota tracking
INCR + EXPIRE is the standard pattern; Cluster for high throughput
FastAPI / middleware
Policy enforcement layer
Dependency injection pattern works well; can be extracted to a shared library
OPA (Open Policy Agent)
Complex policy logic
Better for complex multi-condition policies; Rego is expressive but has a learning curve
Stripe Billing
Cost metering
Meter events API records usage; billing dashboard built-in
Datadog / Grafana
Usage dashboards
Alert on quota exhaustion, usage spikes; visualize usage by tier
AWS WAF / Cloudflare
Network-level rate limiting
Complementary to application-level; blocks volumetric attacks before they reach the app

Security & Reliability

Usage policies are a security control, not just a billing control. An attacker who bypasses rate limits can trigger expensive LLM calls, exhaust the system for other users, or use the agent as a proxy for large-scale data extraction. Protect the policy enforcement layer with: authentication on all endpoints (no unauthenticated bypass), secure Redis connection (TLS + AUTH), and monitoring for policy enforcement failures.

If Redis is unavailable (network partition, Redis failure), the policy engine must make a safe default decision. For most systems, the safe default is to deny all requests until Redis is available. This fails safe but degraded — all users are blocked. An alternative is to fail open with a conservative in-memory rate limit, trading the risk of over-usage for availability.

Policy bypass through tier confusion attacks — sending requests claiming a higher-tier identity — must be prevented by deriving the user's tier from a server-side source of truth (the user database or JWT claims verified with the correct signing key), never from client-provided parameters.
Mental Model

Think of usage policies as the bouncer and the meter at an amusement park. The bouncer (scope check) checks the ticket type: free-tier visitors cannot enter the VIP rides. The meter (rate limit) tracks how many rides you've taken this hour and stops you if you've hit the limit. The cashier (cost limit) tracks your total spend and cuts off your wristband when your prepaid balance runs out. These checks happen simultaneously at the gate before every ride — you do not get to start the ride and pay after.
Key Takeaways
Usage policies enforce four distinct dimensions simultaneously: rate/quota, scope (tool access), cost budgets, and content restrictions — each requires separate enforcement logic
Implement policy checks as middleware that wraps every tool dispatch; this ensures no tool call bypasses enforcement regardless of how the call originates
Use Redis with atomic INCR + EXPIRE for distributed rate limiting; in-memory-only counters fail in multi-instance deployments
Always fail safe: if the policy engine is unavailable, deny requests rather than fail open, to prevent unbounded resource consumption
Expose usage data to users via a dashboard or API; transparency reduces support load and user frustration when limits are hit
Log every policy decision (allow and deny) for audit, tuning, and anomaly detection — usage logs reveal misuse patterns that content checks miss
